
### renaming with microcode 

<!-- \lstset{language=myasm,style=small,morekeywords=iaddq} -->
   

::: {.r-stack}
![](/ooo/texfig/pushqMicrocode.figure-1.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=1}

![](/ooo/texfig/pushqMicrocode.figure-2.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=2}


:::


### renaming and condition codes 

<!-- \lstset{language=myasm,style=small} -->
   

::: {.r-stack}
![](/ooo/texfig/renameCC.figure-1.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=1}

![](/ooo/texfig/renameCC.figure-2.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=2}

![](/ooo/texfig/renameCC.figure-3.svg){.fragment .fade-in-then-out fragment-index=3}


:::


### renaming trickiness (1)  {.smaller}


* need to expose input + outputs
* hidden dependencies on stack pointer, condition codes, memory, etc.
* stack pointer + condition codes 

   * turn into visible register somehow
   * alternative: force to execute in-order

* memory: complex techniques we won't discuss


### renaming trickiness (2)  {.smaller}


* opportunity to translate complex instructions into simpler
* commonly used for memory operands, probably some stack instructions 

   * `popq %rcx` $\rightarrow$ addq, store
   * `addq %rax, (%rbx)` $\rightarrow$ load, addq, store
   * …
